summary: Joel Spolsky argues that Netscape made the worst strategic mistake a software company can make by deciding to rewrite their browser code from scratch, resulting in a three-year gap between releases that destroyed their market position. He explains that programmers chronically undervalue existing code because reading code is harder than writing it, leading them to dismiss working codebases as messy. The 'mess' in old code usually represents hard-won bug fixes and real-world knowledge accumulated over years. Spolsky contends that architectural problems, performance issues, and ugly code can all be fixed incrementally without discarding the entire codebase. He concludes that a full rewrite gives competitors a multi-year gift while providing no guarantee the new version will be better than the original.
key_points: It's harder to read code than to write it, which is the fundamental reason programmers always want to rewrite rather than improve existing code.
Old code that looks messy has typically accumulated bug fixes representing weeks of real-world usage and debugging — rewriting throws away all that knowledge.
The three categories of code problems — architectural issues, inefficiency, and ugliness — can all be addressed incrementally without a full rewrite.
A ground-up rewrite paralyzes a company strategically: you can't ship new features or respond to market changes for years while rebuilding what you already had.
There is no reason to believe a rewrite will produce better code — you'll likely repeat old mistakes and introduce new ones, often with a different team that lacks the original context.
money_quotes: They decided to rewrite the code from scratch.
It's harder to read code than to write it.
The idea that new code is better than old is patently absurd. Old code has been used. It has been tested. Lots of bugs have been found, and they've been fixed.
When you throw away code and start from scratch, you are throwing away all that knowledge. All those collected bug fixes. Years of programming work.
You are giving a gift of two or three years to your competitors, and believe me, that is a long time in software years.
There is absolutely no reason to believe that you are going to do a better job than you did the first time.
We're programmers. Programmers are, in their hearts, architects, and the first thing they want to do when they get to a site is to bulldoze the place flat and build something grand.
key_insight: Rewriting software from scratch is almost always a catastrophic mistake because old code embodies irreplaceable knowledge in the form of accumulated bug fixes, and the problems programmers see as reasons to rewrite can nearly always be solved incrementally.
